text: Jacob lived in Canaan, where his father had lived as a foreigner.
This is Jacob's family history. Joseph, 17, tended flocks with his brothers and reported their bad behavior to their father.
Israel loved Joseph more than his other sons since he was born in his old age, and made him a special robe.
Seeing their father favored Joseph, his brothers hated him and could not speak kindly to him.
Joseph had a dream and told his brothers, which made them hate him even more.
He said, listen to this dream I had.
We were binding sheaves in the field, and my sheaf stood up while yours gathered around and bowed to it.
His brothers said, will you really rule over us? They hated him even more for his dream and words.
Joseph had another dream and told it: the sun, moon, and eleven stars bowed down to me.
When he told his father too, Jacob rebuked him, asking if he, his mother, and brothers would really bow to him.
His brothers were jealous, but his father kept the matter in mind.
His brothers went to graze their father's flocks near Shechem.
Israel said to Joseph, your brothers are at Shechem; go and see how they and the flocks are doing. Joseph agreed.
Jacob sent him from Hebron; Joseph arrived at Shechem.
A man found Joseph wandering and asked what he was looking for.
Joseph said he was looking for his brothers and their flocks.
The man said they had moved on to Dothan. Joseph followed and found them there.
Seeing him from a distance, they plotted to kill him.
They said, here comes the dreamer.
Let's kill him and throw him in a pit, and say a wild animal devoured him; we'll see what becomes of his dreams.
Reuben heard this and tried to save him from their hands, saying not to take his life.
Reuben said, don't shed blood; throw him into this pit instead, planning secretly to rescue him later.
When Joseph reached his brothers, they stripped him of his ornamented robe.
They threw him into an empty cistern with no water in it.
As they sat to eat, they saw a caravan of Ishmaelites coming from Gilead with spices, bound for Egypt.
Judah said, what profit is it to kill our brother and hide his blood?
Let's sell him to the Ishmaelites instead of harming him; he is our brother. His brothers agreed.
Midianite traders pulled Joseph from the cistern and sold him for twenty pieces of silver to the Ishmaelites, who took him to Egypt.
Reuben returned to the cistern and found Joseph gone; he tore his clothes in distress.
He went to his brothers saying, the boy is gone; what will I do?
They took Joseph's robe, slaughtered a goat, and dipped the robe in its blood.
They sent the robe to their father, saying they found it and asking if it was Joseph's.
Jacob recognized it and cried that a wild animal had devoured his son Joseph.
Jacob tore his clothes, put on sackcloth, and mourned his son many days.
All his children tried to comfort him, but he refused, saying he would mourn until he joined his son in death.
Meanwhile the Midianites sold Joseph in Egypt to Potiphar, Pharaoh's official and captain of the guard.
